title: Ethology
expanded_description: An introduction to ethology—the scientific study of animal behavior in natural environments. This guide explains the difference between innate (instinctive) and learned behaviors, how animals are born with certain instinctive behaviors triggered by specific signals, how animals learn through experience, and why understanding both the immediate cause and the evolutionary reason for behavior gives us a complete picture.

core_concept.principle: Ethology studies animal behavior by asking two types of questions: proximate (how does it work?) and ultimate (why did it evolve?). Innate behaviors are instinctive—animals are born knowing how to do them. Learned behaviors are acquired through experience. Most behaviors are a mix of both—animals are born with instincts that are shaped by learning.
core_concept.explanation:
Why do birds build nests? Why do dogs wag their tails? Why do salmon return to the same stream to spawn? Ethology is the science that seeks to answer these questions by observing animals in their natural environments.

Ethologists ask two kinds of questions:
1. Proximate questions: How does it work? What triggers the behavior? What hormones are involved? How does the animal learn it?
2. Ultimate questions: Why did it evolve? How does this behavior help the animal survive and reproduce?

Behaviors can be divided into two broad categories:
• Innate behaviors: Instinctive. Animals are born knowing how to do them. No learning required. Examples: a spider spinning a web, a baby sucking, a bird migrating south.
• Learned behaviors: Acquired through experience. Animals learn from their environment and from other animals. Examples: a dog learning to sit, a chimpanzee using a stick to get termites, a bird learning a local song dialect.

Most behaviors are not purely one or the other—they are a combination. Birds are born with the instinct to sing, but they learn the specific song of their species from listening to adults. This mix of instinct and learning is what makes animal behavior so fascinating.

Fun fact: Konrad Lorenz, one of the founders of ethology, imprinted a group of geese to follow him. The geese thought he was their mother and followed him everywhere—even into the water!

image.alt: Diagram showing innate behavior (spider web) and learned behavior (chimpanzee using stick). Two questions: Proximate (how?) and Ultimate (why?).
image.caption: Ethology: Proximate questions (how does it work?) + Ultimate questions (why did it evolve?) + Innate behaviors (instinct) + Learned behaviors (experience) = complete understanding of animal behavior.

sections.section_title: Introduction: Why Do Animals Do What They Do?
sections.introduction:
Why does a cat stalk a mouse? Why do birds build nests? Why do salmon swim thousands of miles to the stream where they were born? These are questions ethologists ask.

Ethology is the scientific study of animal behavior in natural environments. It was pioneered by scientists like Konrad Lorenz, Niko Tinbergen, and Karl von Frisch, who shared the 1973 Nobel Prize for their discoveries about animal behavior.

Ethologists ask two kinds of questions:
• Proximate questions: What triggers the behavior? How does it work? What hormones or nerves are involved? Does the animal learn it?
• Ultimate questions: Why did the behavior evolve? How does it help the animal survive and reproduce?

Both questions are important. If you only ask how, you know the mechanism but not the purpose. If you only ask why, you know the purpose but not how it works. Together, they give a complete picture.

Behaviors fall into two broad categories:
• Innate behaviors: Instinctive. Animals are born knowing how to do them. No learning required.
• Learned behaviors: Acquired through experience. Animals learn from their environment and from others.

In this section, we'll explore both kinds of behavior, how animals learn, and how scientists figure out why animals behave the way they do.

introduction_image.alt: Tinbergen's four questions: Proximate (causation, development) and Ultimate (function, evolution). Simple examples for each.
introduction_image.caption: Tinbergen's questions: Proximate (how?)—causation (what triggers it?) and development (how does it develop?). Ultimate (why?)—function (how does it help?) and evolution (how did it evolve?).

key_concept.definition: Ethology studies animal behavior by asking proximate questions (how it works) and ultimate questions (why it evolved). Innate behaviors are instinctive; learned behaviors come from experience. Most behaviors combine both.
key_concept.context:
When you see a bird building a nest, you can ask: Proximate questions: What triggers nest-building? (Day length, hormones). How does she learn to build it? (She doesn't—it's instinctive). Ultimate questions: Why does she build a nest? (To protect eggs from predators and weather). How did nest-building evolve? (Ancestral birds that built better nests had more surviving offspring).

Real-world connection: Understanding both proximate and ultimate causes helps us solve problems. For example, understanding why birds collide with windows (proximate: they see reflections as open space) and why they migrate (ultimate: to follow food) helps us design better conservation strategies.

image.alt: Continuum: Reflex (purely innate) → Imprinting (innate + learning) → Tool use (mostly learned).
image.caption: Innate vs. Learned continuum: Some behaviors are pure instinct (reflexes). Some are instinctive but shaped by learning (bird songs). Some are almost entirely learned (tool use).

sections.key_concept: Innate behaviors are instinctive—animals are born with them. Learned behaviors come from experience. Many behaviors combine both: animals are born with instincts that are fine-tuned by learning.

classifications.value: Understanding behaviors animals are born knowing how to do
characteristics: Inherited: Passed down through genes, not learned
Stereotyped: Performed the same way every time
Species-typical: All members of the species do it
Triggered: Set off by specific signals (sign stimuli)
Ballistic: Once started, runs to completion
classifications.behavior:
Innate behaviors are the animal equivalent of a reflex, but more complex. A spider doesn't learn to spin a web—it just knows how. A baby mammal doesn't learn to suckle—it's born with the instinct.

Fixed Action Patterns (FAPs) are complex innate behaviors that are triggered by specific signals and, once started, run to completion. Even if you remove the trigger halfway through, the animal keeps going.

Examples:
• Egg-rolling in geese: If a goose sees an egg outside its nest, it will roll it back in. If you remove the egg mid-roll, the goose continues the rolling motion anyway.
• Stickleback fish courtship: Male sticklebacks develop a red belly during breeding season. The red belly is a signal that triggers aggression in other males and attracts females.
• Bird migration: Many birds know when and where to migrate without being taught.

examples.explanation:
Niko Tinbergen and Konrad Lorenz studied this classic example of a Fixed Action Pattern (FAP):
(1) The trigger: An egg outside the nest. The goose sees the egg.
(2) The behavior: The goose stretches out its neck, places its beak behind the egg, and gently rolls it back into the nest.
(3) The kicker: Once started, the behavior runs to completion. If you remove the egg mid-roll, the goose continues the rolling motion anyway.
(4) The signal: The goose responds to the egg's shape, not its color. It will roll in fake eggs—and even prefers larger, supernormal eggs.

Why does this matter? This shows that some behaviors are hardwired. The goose doesn't think, "There's an egg, I should roll it in." It just does it. The behavior is triggered by a specific signal (the egg shape) and runs automatically.

image.alt: Goose rolling egg; diagram showing removal of egg mid-roll; goose continues motion.
image.caption: Egg-rolling FAP: Trigger (egg shape) → Goose rolls egg → If egg removed, goose continues motion. An instinctive, ballistic behavior.

classifications.value: Understanding the specific signals that trigger instinctive behaviors
characteristics: Sign stimuli: The specific feature that triggers an instinctive behavior
Releaser: A sign stimulus evolved for communication
Supernormal stimulus: An exaggerated sign stimulus that triggers a stronger response
Innate releasing mechanism (IRM): The neural filter that recognizes the sign stimulus
classifications.behavior:
Animals don't respond to the whole world—they respond to specific signals. A male stickleback fish doesn't fight the whole rival fish—it fights the red belly. A herring gull chick doesn't peck at its parent's whole head—it pecks at the red spot on the parent's beak.

These specific triggers are called sign stimuli. They are features that reliably signal something important—a mate, a rival, food, danger.

Supernormal stimuli are exaggerated versions of sign stimuli that trigger even stronger responses. For example, herring gull chicks peck more at models with bigger, redder spots than at their own parent's beak. This reveals that the chick's brain is tuned to respond to the red spot, not to the whole parent.

Why does this happen? Because in nature, bigger, redder spots signal a healthier parent. The chick's instinct evolved to prefer exaggerated signals—which works fine until humans create supernormal stimuli (like fishing lures that look better than real fish).

examples.explanation:
Tinbergen studied how herring gull chicks get fed:
(1) The trigger: The parent gull has a yellow beak with a red spot near the tip.
(2) The behavior: The chick pecks at the red spot. The parent then regurgitates food into the chick's mouth.
(3) Experiment: Tinbergen showed chicks cardboard models of gull heads. Chicks pecked most at models with a red spot—even if the model didn't look like a gull head at all!
(4) Supernormal: A model with three red rings (exaggerated) got more pecks than a normal beak.

Why does this matter? This shows that the chick's brain is tuned to respond to a specific signal (red spot), not to the whole parent. The response is hardwired—it doesn't need to be learned.

image.alt: Gull chick pecking at parent's beak; cardboard models with red spots; supernormal stimulus (three rings).
image.caption: Herring gull chick: Sign stimulus (red spot) triggers pecking. Chicks peck at any red spot—even exaggerated ones (supernormal). Instinctive, not learned.

examples.process: Supernormal Stimuli in Nature and Human Life
examples.explanation:
Supernormal stimuli occur in nature and human life:
(1) Cuckoo chicks: They have exaggerated gapes (mouths) that are wider and redder than their host's own chicks. Host parents feed them more—even though they're not their own young!
(2) Jewel beetles: Males try to mate with discarded beer bottles that have the right color, size, and texture—they're supernormal female beetles.
(3) Human examples: Junk food (supernormal sugar/fat), pornography (supernormal sexual stimuli), social media (supernormal social rewards).

Why does this matter? Supernormal stimuli reveal that our brains are tuned to respond to certain features—and modern technology can exploit those features, sometimes in ways that aren't good for us.

image.alt: Cuckoo chick with wide red gape; beetle trying to mate with beer bottle; examples of human supernormal stimuli (junk food, social media).
image.caption: Supernormal stimuli: Cuckoo chicks (exaggerated gape), jewel beetles (beer bottles), human examples (junk food, social media). Exaggerated features exploit our instincts.

classifications.value: Understanding how animals learn from their environment and from each other
characteristics: Experience-dependent: Acquired through interaction with environment
Variable: Different individuals can learn different things
Flexible: Can change with new experience
Costly: Takes time, energy, and can be risky
Adaptive: Allows adjustment to changing conditions
classifications.behavior:
Learned behaviors allow animals to adapt to their specific environment. A bird born in a forest needs to learn different things than one born in a city. Learning gives flexibility that pure instinct cannot provide.

Types of learning:
• Habituation: Learning to ignore unimportant stimuli. Example: A bird learns that a scarecrow isn't dangerous after nothing happens.
• Classical conditioning: Learning that one thing predicts another. Pavlov's dogs learned that a bell meant food was coming.
• Operant conditioning: Learning by trial and error. A rat learns to press a lever to get food; a dog learns that sitting gets a treat.
• Imprinting: Rapid learning during a critical period early in life. Baby geese follow the first moving object they see—normally their mother.
• Observational learning: Learning by watching others. Chimpanzees learn to use sticks to fish for termites by watching others.

examples.explanation:
Konrad Lorenz discovered imprinting when he noticed that newly hatched goslings would follow him around as if he were their mother.
(1) The critical period: Goslings imprint in the first 13-16 hours after hatching. During this window, they will follow the first moving object they see.
(2) The process: Once imprinted, it's permanent. Those geese followed Lorenz for life.
(3) Why it happens: Imprinting ensures that young animals follow their mother (for protection, learning) rather than wandering off.
(4) Sexual imprinting: In some birds, young imprint on their parents and later choose mates that look like them—ensuring they mate with their own species.

Why this matters: Imprinting shows that learning can happen in a very specific window. It's not a blank slate—animals are born ready to learn certain things at certain times.

image.alt: Konrad Lorenz with goslings following him; diagram of critical period window.
image.caption: Imprinting: Goslings follow the first moving object they see during a critical period (13-16 hours after hatching). Once imprinted, it's permanent.

examples.explanation:
Ivan Pavlov discovered classical conditioning by accident while studying digestion in dogs.
(1) The setup: Pavlov noticed that dogs would start salivating when they saw the lab assistant who fed them—before they saw the food.
(2) The experiment: He rang a bell before giving food. After several pairings, the dogs salivated when they heard the bell alone.
(3) The learning: The dogs learned that the bell predicted food. The bell became a conditioned stimulus.
(4) Why it matters: Classical conditioning is how animals (including humans) learn to predict events. A bird learns that a certain call means a predator is coming. A deer learns that a certain sound means hunters are near.

Fun fact: The same principle explains why your mouth waters when you smell your favorite food cooking—you've learned that smell means food is coming.

image.alt: Pavlov's dogs: Bell before food; dog salivates at bell alone. Diagram of conditioning process.
image.caption: Classical conditioning: Learn that one thing predicts another. Pavlov's dogs learned that bell = food. Your mouth watering at a smell is classical conditioning.

principles.principle: Tinbergen's Four Questions Give a Complete Picture
principles.statement: To fully understand any behavior, you must ask four questions: What triggers it? (causation), How does it develop? (development), How does it help the animal survive and reproduce? (function), and How did it evolve? (evolution). Each question gives a different piece of the puzzle.
principles.application:
This framework explains why:
• Birds sing in spring (causation: longer days trigger hormones; function: attract mates).
• Salmon return to their birthplace (causation: smell memory; evolution: ensures they return to safe spawning grounds).
• Dogs wag their tails (causation: neural signals; function: communication).

**Everyday example**: Why does a baby cry? Causation: hunger or discomfort triggers the cry. Development: babies are born with this reflex. Function: it gets the parent's attention. Evolution: babies who cried got fed and survived.
principles.example:
Bird song analyzed with Tinbergen's questions:
• Causation: Longer days increase testosterone, which activates the song-control areas in the brain.
• Development: Young birds learn their species' song by listening to adults during a critical period.
• Function: Males sing to attract females and warn other males away.
• Evolution: Bird songs evolved from simpler calls; different species have different songs that help them recognize their own kind.

Each answer is valid—and together they give a complete understanding.
principles.analogy: Tinbergen's questions are like asking about a car: How does it work? (engine, transmission). How was it built? (assembly line). What does it do? (gets you places). How did it evolve? (from horse-drawn carriages). All are true—all are needed.

image.alt: Bird song with four boxes: Causation (hormones, brain), Development (learning), Function (mate attraction), Evolution (from simpler calls).
image.caption: Tinbergen's Four Questions applied to bird song: Proximate (causation, development) + Ultimate (function, evolution) = complete understanding.

principles.statement: Most behaviors are not purely innate or purely learned—they combine both. Animals are born with instincts that tell them what to learn, and they learn from experience to fine-tune those instincts.
principles.application:
This explains why:
• Birds are born with the instinct to sing, but they must hear their species' song to learn the right tune.
• Baby sea turtles are born with the instinct to crawl toward the ocean, but they learn the landmarks to return later.
• Humans are born with the capacity for language, but we must hear language to learn it.

**Everyday example**: You were born with the instinct to eat, but you had to learn what's safe to eat and what's not. Instinct + learning = survival.
principles.example:
Bird song learning:
• Instinct: Young birds have an innate auditory template—they know what their species' song should sound like in a general way.
• Learning: They listen to adult males and adjust their own song to match the local dialect.
• Result: They sing a species-typical song with local variations.

If a bird is raised in isolation, it will sing a very basic version of its species' song—but never the full, complex version. Both instinct and experience are necessary.
principles.analogy: Instinct is like a recipe; learning is like cooking experience. The recipe tells you the ingredients, but you learn how to combine them perfectly through practice.

image.alt: Bird singing: Instinct (born with song ability) + Learning (hears adults) = Full song. Diagram showing isolated bird (basic song) vs. normal bird (complex song).
image.caption: Instinct + Learning: Birds are born with the instinct to sing, but they learn the details from adults. Both are needed.

principles.principle: Sign Stimuli Reveal How Animals See the World
principles.statement: Animals don't perceive the world as we do. They are tuned to specific signals (sign stimuli) that matter to their survival. Understanding these signals helps us understand animal behavior—and sometimes, our own.
principles.application:
This explains why:
• Male stickleback fish fight red bellies, not whole fish.
• Gull chicks peck red spots, not whole beaks.
• Humans are attracted to exaggerated features (big eyes, smooth skin) that signal health.

**Everyday example**: Why do fishing lures work? They are supernormal stimuli—they exaggerate features of real prey to trigger a strike response. Fish don't think, "That looks tasty"—they react instinctively.
principles.example:
The male three-spined stickleback develops a red belly during breeding season. This red belly is a sign stimulus that triggers two responses:
• Aggression in other males: A red belly means "rival." Males attack any red object—even a crude model.
• Attraction in females: A red belly means "good mate." Females prefer males with brighter red bellies.

This shows how a simple signal can evolve to communicate two different messages. The signal works because receivers are hardwired to respond to it.
principles.analogy: Sign stimuli are like traffic lights. You don't need to understand traffic engineering to know that red means stop. Your brain is hardwired to respond. Animals have their own "traffic lights" for danger, food, and mates.

image.alt: Male stickleback with red belly; crude red model triggering aggression; female preferring brighter red.
image.caption: Sign stimulus: Red belly triggers aggression in males, attraction in females. A simple signal evolved for communication.

components.definition: Ethology integrates the study of innate behaviors (fixed action patterns, sign stimuli), learned behaviors (classical conditioning, operant conditioning, imprinting, observational learning), and the Tinbergen framework (proximate and ultimate causes).
components.equation: Complete understanding = Proximate causes + Ultimate causes
components.interpretation: To fully understand a behavior, you need to know both how it works (proximate) and why it evolved (ultimate). One without the other is incomplete.

image.alt: Diagram: Innate (FAPs, sign stimuli) + Learned (conditioning, imprinting) + Tinbergen's Questions = Complete picture.
image.caption: Key components: Innate behaviors (instincts), Learned behaviors (experience), and Tinbergen's Four Questions (how? why?). All needed to understand animal behavior.

categories.description: Some animals learn by watching others. This can lead to cultural traditions—behaviors passed from one generation to the next through learning, not genes.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
Observational learning: An animal learns by watching the behavior of another animal. This is how chimpanzees learn to use sticks to fish for termites, and how young birds learn songs from adults.

Culture in animals: When behaviors spread through social learning and persist across generations, it's called culture. Chimpanzees in different regions use different tools—they have distinct tool-using cultures. Whales have different songs in different oceans; new songs spread like fads. Japanese macaques learned to wash sweet potatoes from one innovative female; the behavior spread through the troop and was passed to offspring.

examples.explanation:
Jane Goodall's chimpanzee research revealed tool use and culture:
(1) Tool use: Chimps use sticks to "fish" for termites; they use rocks to crack nuts. Different populations use different tools.
(2) Learning: Young chimps watch adults and practice for years before mastering the skills.
(3) Culture: Different chimp populations have different tool kits, grooming behaviors, and even different ways of eating. These are not genetic—they're learned traditions.

Why this matters: Tool use was once thought to be uniquely human. Chimpanzees show that the capacity for culture and tool use is shared with our closest relatives.

image.alt: Chimpanzee using stick to fish for termites; young chimp watching; map of different tool-using populations.
image.caption: Observational learning: Chimpanzees learn tool use by watching others. Different populations have different "cultures"—tool types, grooming styles, food preferences.

categories.description: Animals communicate using signals—sounds, visual displays, chemicals, and behaviors. Signals are shaped by evolution to be honest (or at least, they must be reliable enough).
categories.detailed_mechanism:
Communication signals can be:
• Visual: Color displays (peacock tail), body postures (dominance), facial expressions
• Auditory: Bird songs, whale songs, alarm calls
• Chemical: Pheromones (ants, moths, mammals)
• Tactile: Grooming, touching

Honest signals: Signals that are costly or impossible to fake. A peacock's tail is expensive to grow and carry—only a healthy male can afford it. The tail is an honest signal of quality.

examples.explanation:
Vervet monkeys have distinct alarm calls for different predators:
(1) Eagle call: Monkeys look up, run into bushes.
(2) Leopard call: Monkeys run up trees.
(3) Snake call: Monkeys stand on two legs, look down.

Experiments showed that when researchers played recorded calls, monkeys reacted appropriately—even with no predator present. This shows that the calls are meaningful signals, not just emotional screams.

Why this matters: Vervet monkeys have something like words—symbolic signals that refer to specific things. This was once thought to be uniquely human.

image.alt: Vervet monkeys with eagle, leopard, snake; different responses for each call. Researchers playing recorded calls.
image.caption: Animal communication: Vervet monkeys have different alarm calls for eagles, leopards, and snakes. They respond appropriately—even to recorded calls. This shows symbolic communication.

worked_examples.title: Case Study: Male Stickleback Courtship
worked_examples.problem: Male three-spined stickleback fish develop a red belly during breeding season. They perform a zigzag dance to attract females and lead them to the nest. Analyze this behavior using Tinbergen's Four Questions.

step_by_step.calculation: Longer days (spring) trigger hormone changes. Testosterone increases, causing the male to develop a red belly. The red belly is the sign stimulus. When the male sees a female, he performs the zigzag dance—a fixed action pattern triggered by the female's swollen belly.
The behavior is innate. Young males raised in isolation will still develop a red belly and perform the zigzag dance when mature. No learning is required—it's hardwired.

The red belly signals that the male is ready to mate. Females prefer brighter red males—they are healthier and have better territories. The zigzag dance leads the female to the nest, where she lays eggs. The male then guards the eggs. All of this increases the male's reproductive success.

The red belly evolved as an honest signal of male quality. The zigzag dance likely evolved from nest-building movements. Related fish species have similar courtship behaviors, suggesting a common ancestor.

worked_examples.final_answer: Causation: Hormones trigger red belly; female triggers dance. Development: Innate—no learning needed. Function: Attracts females; leads to mating and egg guarding. Evolution: Red belly as honest signal; dance derived from nest-building. All four questions needed for complete understanding.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Tinbergen's framework shows that behavior can be studied at multiple levels. Each level answers a different question, and together they give a complete picture.

sections.section_title: Process Flow: How a Fixed Action Pattern Works
sections.introduction: A Fixed Action Pattern (FAP) is an innate behavior triggered by a specific signal and running to completion once started.

process_flow.description: Animal detects a specific key feature in the environment.
process_flow.mechanism: Sensory organs (eyes, ears, etc.) detect the sign stimulus. Example: Goose sees egg-shaped object outside nest.
process_flow.result: Signal sent to brain; innate releasing mechanism activated

image.alt: Goose seeing egg outside nest.
image.caption: Step 1: Sign stimulus detected. The goose sees an egg-shaped object outside the nest.

process_flow.description: The brain recognizes the sign stimulus and triggers the behavior.
process_flow.mechanism: A neural filter (innate releasing mechanism) responds to the sign stimulus and activates the motor program.

image.alt: Brain recognizing egg shape; activating motor program.
image.caption: Step 2: Innate releasing mechanism activates. The brain recognizes the sign stimulus and triggers the motor program.

process_flow.description: The behavior runs to completion, even if the stimulus disappears.
process_flow.mechanism: Central pattern generator produces coordinated motor output. The sequence is ballistic—once started, it continues.

image.alt: Goose rolling egg; even if egg removed, goose continues motion.
image.caption: Step 3: Fixed action pattern executes. The behavior runs to completion. Even if the egg is removed, the goose continues the rolling motion.

sections.definition: These are more advanced concepts about how scientists study animal behavior, including how they map brain activity during behavior and how genes influence instincts.

process_types.energy_profile: Neuroethology studies the neural circuits that produce behavior. Scientists can now map which brain areas are active during specific behaviors.
process_types.direction: Observe behavior → Record brain activity → Identify active brain regions → Manipulate circuits → Test behavior
process_types.function: Understand how the brain controls instinctive and learned behaviors
goals: Learn that specific brain areas control specific behaviors
Understand how scientists study the brain during behavior
Appreciate that instincts have a neural basis

primary_example.explanation:
Songbirds have specialized brain regions for singing:
(1) HVC: Helps produce and learn songs. (2) RA: Sends signals to the muscles that control the syrinx (bird's voice box). (3) Area X: Involved in learning songs from adults.

Young birds must hear adult songs during a critical period. If they don't, they never learn to sing properly. The brain is hardwired to learn songs—but the details are filled in by experience.

Why this matters: This shows how instinct (the drive to sing) and learning (the specific song) combine. The brain is built to learn—it's not a blank slate.

image.alt: Bird brain diagram showing HVC, RA, Area X; song spectrogram; critical period graph.
image.caption: Bird song brain: Specialized areas (HVC, RA, Area X) control song learning and production. Instinct + learning = species-typical song.

image.alt: Electrodes recording from brain; brain activity map during behavior; manipulating neurons with light (optogenetics).
image.caption: Neuroethology: Studying the brain during behavior. Scientists can record which neurons fire, and even turn them on and off to see what happens.

process_types.energy_profile: Some behaviors are influenced by specific genes. Scientists can identify genes that affect behaviors like courtship, aggression, and learning.
process_types.direction: Observe behavioral variation → Find genes linked to variation → Manipulate genes → Test behavior
process_types.function: Understand the genetic basis of instinctive behaviors
goals: Understand that most behaviors are influenced by many genes
Appreciate that instinct is coded in DNA

primary_example.process: The fruitless Gene and Fruit Fly Courtship
primary_example.explanation:
Male fruit flies perform a complex courtship dance to attract females. They orient toward the female, tap her with a leg, extend a wing and sing a song, and try to mate.

The fruitless gene controls this behavior. Male flies with a mutation in fruitless don't court females—they court males instead. Remarkably, if scientists put the male version of the fruitless gene into female flies, they perform male courtship dances!

Why this matters: This shows that a single gene can control a complex behavior. It's not that the gene codes for "courtship"—it helps build the brain circuits that produce courtship behavior. The behavior is built into the brain's wiring.

image.alt: Male fruit fly courting female; mutant male courting male; female with male gene courting female.
image.caption: The fruitless gene: Controls male courtship behavior. Mutant males court males; females with the male version of the gene court females. A single gene influences complex behavior.

image.alt: DNA helix; behavior differences; gene mapping; mouse with altered gene.
image.caption: Behavioral genetics: Genes influence behavior. Some behaviors are influenced by single genes; most are influenced by many genes interacting with environment.

sections.relationship: Ethology is the study of animal behavior in natural settings. It asks two kinds of questions: proximate (how does it work?) and ultimate (why did it evolve?). Innate behaviors (fixed action patterns) are instinctive—animals are born knowing how to do them. They are triggered by specific signals (sign stimuli) and run to completion once started. Learned behaviors come from experience—classical conditioning (Pavlov's dogs), operant conditioning (trial and error), imprinting (Lorenz's geese), and observational learning (chimpanzee tool use). Most behaviors combine instinct and learning: birds are born with the drive to sing, but they learn the details of their species' song. Tinbergen's Four Questions provide a framework for complete behavioral analysis: causation (what triggers it?), development (how does it develop?), function (how does it help survival/reproduction?), and evolution (how did it evolve?). Neuroethology studies the brain circuits behind behavior; behavioral genetics finds genes that influence behavior. Together, these approaches give us a complete picture of why animals do what they do.

summary:
Ethology is the scientific study of animal behavior in natural environments. It was pioneered by Konrad Lorenz, Niko Tinbergen, and Karl von Frisch.

Two kinds of questions:
• Proximate questions: How does the behavior work? What triggers it? How does it develop?
• Ultimate questions: Why did the behavior evolve? How does it help the animal survive and reproduce?

Innate behaviors (instincts):
• Animals are born knowing how to do them. No learning required.
• Fixed Action Patterns (FAPs): Stereotyped innate behaviors triggered by specific signals. Once started, they run to completion. Example: goose rolling egg back into nest.
• Sign stimuli: The specific signals that trigger FAPs. Example: red belly triggers stickleback aggression; red spot triggers gull chick pecking.
• Supernormal stimuli: Exaggerated sign stimuli that trigger stronger responses. Example: cuckoo chick's exaggerated gape; beetles trying to mate with beer bottles.

Learned behaviors:
• Acquired through experience.
• Classical conditioning: Learning that one thing predicts another (Pavlov's dogs).
• Operant conditioning: Learning by trial and error (Skinner's rats).
• Imprinting: Rapid learning during a critical period early in life (Lorenz's geese).
• Observational learning: Learning by watching others (chimpanzee tool use).

Most behaviors combine instinct and learning: birds are born with the instinct to sing, but they learn the details of their species' song from adults.

Tinbergen's Four Questions:
1. Causation: What triggers the behavior? What hormones, nerves, etc., are involved?
2. Development: How does the behavior develop in the individual? Does it require learning?
3. Function: How does the behavior help the animal survive and reproduce?
4. Evolution: How did the behavior evolve? What did it look like in ancestors?

Examples:
• Stickleback courtship: Red belly (sign stimulus) triggers aggression in males, attracts females. Behavior is innate (no learning), increases reproductive success, evolved from simpler ancestral behaviors.
• Bird song: Triggered by hormones; learned during critical period; functions to attract mates and defend territory; evolved from simpler calls.
• Vervet monkey alarm calls: Different calls for eagles, leopards, snakes. Monkeys respond appropriately—evidence of symbolic communication.

Advanced topics:
• Neuroethology: Studies brain circuits that control behavior. Songbirds have specialized brain regions (HVC, RA) for singing.
• Behavioral genetics: Genes influence behavior. The fruitless gene controls male fruit fly courtship. Mutants court males instead of females; females with the male gene court females.

Understanding animal behavior helps us understand our own behavior—and why animals (including humans) do what they do.
